content.full_text:
Re
Fnrn Polrrrcal Pnacucps CotrvussroN
P.O. Box li07 . .+2lt J Srreet . Sacramenro. CA 9-51il2-0g07
1916) 322--5660 . Fax (916) -122-Oliti6
December 28,2000
Greg Hons dba DNA
P.O. Box 40452
Santa Barbara, CA 93140
Your Request for Informal Advice
Our File No. I-00-255
Dear Mr. Hons:
We advise only on the requirements of the Political Reform Act. We encourage
you to consider whether the doctrine of "Incompatible Activities" or Government Code
section 1090 issues, which prohibit govemment officials fu'om having an interest in
contracts with their own agency, are triggered by your facts. The Attorney General,s
office has jurisdiction over both of these areas of law.
QUESTION
1. You are a police officer for the Citv of Santa Barbara, may you apply to be on
the City's "approved vendor list" in order to sell equipment from your businlri to th"
police department?
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the'oAct").1 BecausJyour question is generafin nut*. and does
not pertain to a specific govefirmental decisioll, we are treating your request as one for
general assistance. (Regulation 1 8 3 29(b)(c).)2
' Government Code sections 81000 - 91015. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18^996, of the California Code of Regulations.
2 Infonnal assistance does not p.oiid" the requestor with the immunity provided in an opinion or
formal written advice. (Section 83 I 14; Regulation 18329(c).) Further, please noti that the Commission is
cunently considering amendments to the conflict-of-interest regulationi, which may affect the analysis
contained in this letter. You can monitor the progress of these changes at the Commission's Website
(www.fppc.ca.gov).


Our File No. I-00-255
Page 2
2. Once on the "approved vendor list" may you sell equipment to the police
department?
CONCLUSION
1. You may apply to be on the approved vendor list so long as you do not attempt
to influence the decision by purporting to act on behalf of, or as a representative of the
police department.
2. You may sell equipment to the police deparhnent so long as you do not attempt
to influence the decision by contacting or appearing before any member, officer,
employee, or consultant of the police department. Although prohibited from influencing
any decision, you may act in the same manner as any other member of the general public
in order to represent your private interest in your business.
FACTS
You are a police officer for the City of Santa Barbara. You are assigned to patrol
duties and have no responsibility for purchasing decisions made by the police
department. You do not lobby anyone with the power to make purchasing decisions. In
your private capacity, you own a small business, which sells firearms and sporting
equipment. You are the sole proprietor of the business. You would like to sell
equipment to the police department and in order to do so, must be on the City's approved
vendor list.
ANALYSIS
A public official may not make, participate in making, or in any way attempt to
use his or her official position to influence a govemmental decision in which the official
knows or has reason to know, that he or she has a financial interest. (Section 87100).
The Commission has adopted a standard analysis for deciding whether an official has a-
disqualifying conflict of interest. (Regulation 18700, subdivisions (b)(1) - (g).)3
1. Public Official
The conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to public officials. (Section 87100)
As an employee for the police department for the City of Santa Barbara you are a public
official subject to the prohibition. (Section 82048.)
' We have provided an analysis of only steps I and 2 because the facts you have provided do not
indicate that you will be making, participating in making or influencing a govemmental deiision. If, in the
fu1ure, you feel you might be inlluencing a governmental decision yor-*y write in again for more
information on the other 6 steps.


Our File No. I-00-255
Page 3
2. Conduct Covered
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only where a public official is
making, participating in making, or is in any way attempting to use his or her official
position to influence a govemmental decision in which he or she knows or has reason to
know the official has a financial interest. (Section 87100.)
The Commission has adopted a series of regulations which define "making,"
"participating in making," and "influencing" a government decision, and which also
provide certain exceptions. (Regulations 1 8702- 187 02.4.)
A public official "makes a governmental decision," when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, appoints a person,
obligates or commits his or her agency to any course of action, enters into any contractual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency, or determines not to do any of these things,
unless such determination is made because of his or her financial interest. (Regulation
r8702.t(a)(1)-(s).)
A public official'participates in making a governmental decision," when, acting
within the authority of his or her position the official:
"(a) Negotiates, without significant substantive review, with a governmental
entity or private person regarding a govemmental decision...
(b) Advises or makes recorrmendations to the decisionmaker either directly
or without significant intervening substantive review, by:
(1) Conducting research or making any investigation which requires the
exercise ofjudgment on the part of the official and the purpose of
which is to influence a governmental decisior...", or by
"(2) Preparing or presenting any report, analysis, or opinion, orally, or in
writing, which requires the exercise ofjudgment on the part of the official
and the purpose of which is to influence a governmental decision..."
@e gulation I 87 02 .2 (a) (b) .)
Since you indicated in your letter that you will not be making decisions about
purchasing or negotiating, advising or making recommendations to those who make
purchasing decisions, we conclude you will not be making or participating in making a
governmental decision.a
The Commission applies two rules to determine whether a public official is using
or attempting to use his or her official position to "influence" a governmental decision.
The first rule applies when the governmental decision is before the public official's own
n This letter is based only on the facts as they are presented to us. The Commission does not act as the
finder of fact in providing advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71.)


Our File No. I-00-255
Page 4
agency, or an agency appointed by or subject to the budgetary control of the official's
agency. The second applies when the decision is before an agency other than the
official's own agency.
a. The City's Head of Purchasing
For decisions before an agency other than the public official's own agency, or an
agency appointed by or subject to the budgetary control of the official's agency, the
Commission applies a narow prohibition. Based on this rule, an official will only be
"influencing" a decision if the offrcial purports to act on behalf of, or as the
representative of, his or her agency to any member, officer, employee or consultant of
that agency.
Generally, the Commission has treated different city departments as different
agencies. (Stout Advice Letter, No. I-88-313). Specifically, a police department has
been treated as a distinct agency within a city. (Burnett Advice Letter, No. I-90-637).
Therefore, with respect to your application to be placed on the approved vendor list, this
decision will fall within the second rule, and you will be prohibited only from purporting
to act on behalf of, or as the representative of, the police department to any member,
officer, employee or consultant of the agency in charge of making this decision.
b. The Police Department:
With regard to a governmental decision which is before an official's own agency,
the official is attempting to use his or her official position to influence the decision if, for
the purpose of influencing the decision, the official contacts, appears before, or otherwise
attempts to influenco, ffiy member, officer, employee or consultant of the agency.
Attempts to influence include, but are not limited to, appearances or contacts by the
official on behalf of a business entity, client, or customer. (Regulation 18702.3(a).)
The decisions by the police deparhnent about whether to purchase equipment
from your business will be considered a decision before your own agency.
For these decisions you will be subject to the broad prohibition which prevents
you from communicating with any member, officer, employee, or consultant of your
agency about the decision.
However, the Commission has established several exceptions to this rule, one of
which permits an otherwise disqualified official to appear in the same manner as any
other member of the general public before the official's own agency in the course of its
prescribed governmental function to represent his personal interests in a business entity
wholly owned by the official or members of his or her immediate family. (Regulation
18702.4(b).)


Our File No. I-00-255
Page 5
Therefore, even though you might be disqualified from communicating with your
agency with regard to the decisions because, as you stated in your letter, you are the sole
proprietor of your business, you may appear in the same manner as any other member of
the general public before your own agency, in the course of its prescribed govemmental
function to represent your personal interests in your business entity. @egulation
tS7O2.4(b).) This exception would apply only inthose instances where you make it clear
that you are not acting in an official capacity.
We have enclosed a pamphlet that addresses the fuIl conflicts of interest analysis
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, piease contact me at (916) 322-
5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
Assistant General Counsel
(-tiSh
By:
Mikesell
Legal Intern
MAMjg
Enclosure
